14.text: Standard math library for the attention scaling factor.

18.text: PyTorch enters the picture. Everything changes — tensors, autograd, GPU support, all in one package.

19.text: The nn module provides the building blocks: layers, modules, loss functions.

20.text: Functional API for operations like softmax and cross-entropy that don't have learned parameters.

22.text: Same Python seed for identical dataset ordering across all labs.

23.text: PyTorch has its own RNG — seed it separately for reproducible weight initialization.

28.text: Same dataset path — all labs share the data directory.

29.text: Auto-download the names dataset if needed.

32.text: Fetch the dataset from Karpathy's repo.

39.text: BOS token gets the highest ID.

46.text: Same hyperparameters as labs 01-02 — this lab changes the framework, not the architecture.

47.text: 4 heads, each attending to different relationships.

53.text: RMSNorm as an nn.Module — PyTorch's standard way to define reusable layers. Compare to lab 01's bare function.

54.text: Constructor takes dimension (unused here since we have no learnable scale) and epsilon.

58.text: The forward method is what gets called when you use the module. Same math as lab 01, but on tensors.

59.text: One line: compute RMS and scale. rsqrt is reciprocal square root — fused for speed.

62.text: Self-attention as a module. Four weight matrices, all managed by PyTorch.

65.text: nn.Linear wraps a weight matrix with proper initialization, gradient tracking, and GPU support. No bias, matching our architecture.

66.text: Key projection — same as lab 01's attn_wk, but PyTorch tracks gradients automatically.

70.text: Forward pass for attention. Notice: no cache management, no manual gradient logic. Just the math.

71.text: Unpack batch, sequence length, and channel dimensions. The B dimension is new — even though we train on one example, PyTorch expects batch format.

72.text: Project to Q, reshape into heads, and transpose so heads are a batch dimension. Same reshape as lab 02 but with PyTorch tensor ops.

77.text: Scaled dot-product attention — one matrix multiply computes all attention scores. Same formula as lab 01's inner loop, fully vectorized.

78.text: Causal mask: upper triangle of True values. Positions can only attend to earlier positions.

79.text: Fill future positions with -inf so softmax gives them zero weight.

80.text: Softmax over the key dimension — each query gets a probability distribution over which keys to attend to.

82.text: Weighted value sum, transpose heads back, reshape to (B, T, C). The attention mechanism in three operations.

83.text: Output projection combines information from all heads.

86.text: MLP block — the "thinking" part of each transformer layer.

89.text: Expand to 4x dimension: the wider hidden layer provides capacity for more complex transformations.

92.text: Forward: expand, activate, contract. One line replaces lab 01's three-step MLP.

93.text: ReLU + two linear layers. The entire MLP in a single expression.

96.text: A transformer block combines attention and MLP with residual connections and normalization.

104.text: The famous two-line transformer block: normalize-attend-add, normalize-MLP-add.

105.text: Attention with residual connection. Same as lab 01's manual x = [a + b for a, b in zip(x, x_residual)], but expressed as clean addition.

106.text: MLP with residual connection. The entire block is two residual sub-layers.

110.text: The complete GPT model. Four components: embeddings, normalization, transformer layers, and the output head.

113.text: nn.Embedding is a lookup table — same as indexing into lab 01's wte list, but with built-in gradient tracking.

114.text: Position embeddings — learned, not sinusoidal. Each position gets its own vector.

115.text: Initial RMSNorm before the transformer layers.

116.text: ModuleList registers all layers so PyTorch can find their parameters for optimization.

117.text: Output head: project from embedding space to vocabulary logits. No bias, matching our design.

118.text: Apply custom weight initialization to every sub-module.

121.text: Static method — doesn't need self because initialization doesn't depend on the model instance.

122.text: Only initialize Linear and Embedding layers — skip RMSNorm (it has no weights).

125.text: The forward pass. Compare to lab 01: this is the same algorithm, but embedding lookups, matrix multiplies, normalization, and attention are all handled by PyTorch layers.

128.text: Position embedding: arange(T) generates [0, 1, ..., T-1] for position IDs.

129.text: Fuse embeddings, normalize, and enter the transformer.

131.text: Each layer applies attention + MLP with residual connections.

132.text: Project to vocabulary logits — the model's prediction for each position.

138.text: CPU training. For this tiny model, CPU is fine.

139.text: Instantiate and move to device. .to(device) would matter for GPU; here it's a no-op.

142.text: PyTorch's built-in Adam optimizer. One line replaces the manual Adam implementation from labs 01-02. Same hyperparameters: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99).

150.text: Create input tensor with batch dimension. The [...] wrapping adds the batch dimension — PyTorch always expects (B, T) shape.

151.text: Target tensor: same tokens shifted by one position.

153.text: Forward pass. All gradient tracking happens automatically — PyTorch builds the computation graph behind the scenes.

154.text: cross_entropy combines softmax + negative log likelihood in one numerically stable operation. Replaces lab 01's manual softmax + log + negation.

156.text: Zero gradients from previous step. Same as lab 01's p.grad = 0, but for all parameters at once.

157.text: THE magic line. loss.backward() computes all gradients automatically. This one call replaces lab 01's entire Value.backward() engine AND lab 02's 80+ lines of hand-derived gradients.

158.text: Linear learning rate decay — same schedule as previous labs.

159.text: PyTorch groups parameters; we update the learning rate for all groups.

161.text: Apply the Adam update. The optimizer reads the gradients computed by backward() and updates all parameters.

164.text: .item() extracts the Python float from a 0-dim tensor — needed for printing.

171.text: Switch to eval mode — disables dropout (if any). Good practice, though our model doesn't use dropout.

172.text: torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking during inference — saves memory and computation. We're only generating, not training.

176.text: Feed the generated sequence (up to context length) as a batch of 1.

177.text: Forward pass — no gradient tracking needed.

178.text: Take the last position's logits, apply temperature.

180.text: multinomial samples one token from the distribution. PyTorch's equivalent of lab 01's random.choices.

184.text: Convert token IDs to characters, skipping the initial BOS.
